rows.title: Belgium Adopts National Cyber Crisis Response Plan by Royal Decree, Replacing 2017 Framework
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium has formally adopted an updated national cyber crisis response plan by Royal Decree, grounded in the Law of 26 April 2024 transposing the NIS2 Directive into Belgian law and the Law of 25 May 2007 on civil security, replacing the previous framework dating from 2017.

On 27 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published the revised national plan for responding to cyber crises and cybersecurity incidents in the Belgian Official Gazette, following its adoption by Royal Decree. No financial penalties are associated with this measure. The CCB notes that in 2025 it recorded 556 reports of cybersecurity incidents, of which 8 carried national impact, with public administration and the healthcare sector identified as the most frequently targeted, though the CCB states that no sector remains entirely spared.

rows.title: Belarus Centre for Personal Data Protection Issues Guidance on University Website Publication
rows.summary:
The National Centre for Personal Data Protection of the Republic of Belarus has issued formal guidance on the lawful bases and data minimisation requirements for higher education institutions publishing personal data about applicants, students and graduates on their official websites.

On 25 April 2026, the National Centre for Personal Data Protection of the Republic of Belarus published guidance concerning the application of the Law of the Republic of Belarus of 7 May 2021 No. 99-Z "On Personal Data Protection" to online publication of personal data by higher education institutions, coordinated with the Ministry of Education.

The Centre notes that online publication of personal data constitutes the most sensitive form of processing for individuals, owing to the difficulty of complete deletion once data has been disseminated. The guidance addresses eleven specific scenarios, including publishing enrolment lists, institutional and student council composition, information about distinguished graduates, participation in educational events, and dormitory allocation. For enrolment lists, the Centre determines that the legal basis is Article 6, paragraph twenty, of the Law, read together with Article 55(3) of the Education Code. The Centre determines that permissible data in such lists is limited to full name, scores achieved and information on benefits. The Centre notes that benefit entitlement may be indicated by notation such as "benefit" without disclosing the underlying sensitive ground, for example disability or orphan status.

Regarding council membership, the Centre finds that neither the Regulation on the Council of an Educational Institution nor the Regulation on the Student Council requires publication of council composition on institutional websites. The Centre determines that such publication is lawful without consent only where expressly provided for in the higher education institution's charter, with Article 6, paragraph twenty, of the Law serving as the legal basis; otherwise, the Centre finds that the consent of the individuals concerned is required.
rows.body_name: National Centre for Personal Data Protection (Belarus)

rows.title: Belgian DPA and ICO Sign Memorandum of Understanding on Cross-Border Data Protection Cooperation
rows.summary:
The Belgian Data Protection Authority and the United Kingdom's Information Commissioner's Office have entered into a Memorandum of Understanding establishing a framework for cross-border regulatory cooperation in the enforcement and administration of data protection law, with no financial penalty attached to the agreement.

On 22 April 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Belgian DPA) and the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) signed the Memorandum of Understanding for "Cooperation in the Regulation of Laws Protecting Personal Data" at the Privacy Symposium in Venice, Italy. The Belgian DPA and the ICO recognise, as the document states, the complexities of the international circulation and exchange of personal data and the increased need for cross-border cooperation that this creates.

The Belgian DPA and the ICO describe the MoU as a joint statement of intent providing both parties with a framework intended to foster productive and clear cooperation while adhering to their respective legal differences and statutory independence. The Belgian DPA notes that the agreement is intended to streamline regulatory approaches when addressing similar issues and to promote consistency in the administration of comparable data protection laws. The Belgian DPA states that the scope of cooperation identified in the MoU covers consultation on, and taking account of, each party's regulatory activity to better protect individuals within the scope of applicable data protection and privacy laws, as well as supporting organisations in their compliance with personal data protection obligations.

The Belgian DPA states that the MoU sets out broad principles of collaboration, identifying the role and functions of each authority and the scope of their cooperation. The Belgian DPA notes that one priority area is parallel investigations or enforcement actions, and that collaboration may include the sharing of experiences and best practices, as well as the exchange of information, expressly excluding personal data, about potential or ongoing investigations regarding potential contraventions impacting both jurisdictions. The Belgian DPA states that establishing reliable partnerships with other data protection authorities forms a key pillar of its 2026–2028 strategic plan, which also identifies international cooperation, including participation in the European Data Protection Board and bodies such as the Global Privacy Assembly and the GPEN, as a core axis of its institutional priorities.

rows.title: Belgian Data Protection Authority Issues Opinion on Draft Brussels Ordinance Governing Information Exchange on Public Road Infrastructure
rows.summary:
The Belgian Data Protection Authority has issued Opinion 44/2026, adopted on 12 March 2026, raising data protection concerns under the GDPR regarding a draft Brussels-Capital Region ordinance on the exchange of information relating to installations on public roads and regional domain, requested by the Minister for Mobility, Public Works and Road Safety.

On 12 March 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Autorité de protection des données, APD) adopted Opinion 44/2026 in response to a request received on 4 December 2025 from Minister Elke Van den Brandt. The opinion concerns a preliminary draft ordinance intended to replace the Brussels ordinance of 26 July 2013 and the government decree of 16 May 2014, both governing access to and exchange of information on underground cables and conduits. No fine was imposed in the context of this advisory opinion.

The APD identifies three categories of personal data processing that will arise under the draft ordinance: processing within the CICC system (Articles 4 et seq. of the draft), processing in connection with two cadastres managed by Paradigm covering fibre, ducts and physical infrastructures (Article 10 of the draft), and processing in the context of the investigation of infringements and the imposition of administrative sanctions (Articles 13 to 27 of the draft). The APD finds that the requestor did not answer its questions regarding the scope of personal data processing within the CICC system, and notes that the explanatory memorandum attributes responsibility for that processing to the federal legislator rather than addressing it within the draft ordinance itself.

The APD further finds that certain definitions used in the draft ordinance diverge from those in EU Regulation 2024/1309 of 29 April 2024 on measures to reduce the cost of deploying gigabit electronic communications networks, despite the explanatory memorandum stating that the draft aligns with that Regulation's definitions. The APD draws attention specifically to the concepts of "organisme du secteur public" (Art. 2, 14° of the draft) and "civiele werken" in the Dutch-language version (Art. 2, 21° of the draft). The APD also finds that both the text and the explanatory memorandum contain errors that impair legibility, citing among other examples an incorrect reference to a Council of State opinion, and emphasises the importance of clarity and precision in legislative texts as a matter of the principles of legality and foreseeability under the GDPR (Art. 5(1)(a) GDPR).

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Sets 18 April 2026 Compliance Deadline for Essential Entities Under NIS2
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium has confirmed that essential entities operating under the Belgian NIS2 framework face a binding compliance deadline of 18 April 2026, requiring demonstration of active cybersecurity risk-management measures under the Belgian NIS2 Law of 26 April 2024, which entered into force on 18 October 2024.

On 16 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published guidance detailing what essential entities must have in place by 18 April 2026. The CCB states that this deadline derives from a formal request issued by its inspection service, under which all essential entities are required to submit specific information and supporting evidence to enable ex-ante supervision and verification of their cybersecurity posture.

The CCB sets out three compliance pathways, each with distinct requirements. Under the CyberFundamentals (CyFun®) pathway, the CCB finds that entities are required to obtain, or be actively in the process of obtaining, at least a Basic or Important verification, or to hold a signed agreement with an accredited assessment body. Under the ISO/IEC 27001 pathway, the CCB states that entities are required to submit their certification scope, Statement of Applicability and most recent internal audit report by 18 April 2026, with full certification to be completed by April 2027. The CCB notes that entities opting for direct inspection are required to provide a self-assessment and relevant supporting documentation and to formally request an inspection, with the CCB stating that this pathway may lead directly to supervisory measures.

The CCB determines that the 18 April 2026 deadline constitutes a binding regulatory obligation and not a procedural formality. The CCB states that failure to submit complete or timely information may result in administrative measures or financial penalties, as well as further supervisory action. The CCB notes that the Belgian NIS2 Law is designed to strengthen cybersecurity across essential services through risk management, incident reporting and supervision, reflecting the obligations established under the EU NIS2 Directive.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Sets 18 April 2026 Compliance Deadline for Essential Entities Under NIS2
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium has issued a formal inspection request requiring all essential entities operating under the Belgian NIS2 framework to demonstrate active implementation of cybersecurity risk management measures by 18 April 2026, under the Belgian NIS2 Law of 26 April 2024.

On 16 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published guidance confirming that the 18 April 2026 deadline constitutes a binding regulatory obligation for essential entities, not a procedural formality. The CCB states that all essential entities are required to provide specific information and supporting documentation to enable ex ante supervision and verification of their cybersecurity posture, following a formal request issued by the CCB's inspection service.

The CCB sets out three recognised compliance pathways that essential entities are required to follow. Under the CyberFundamentals (CyFun®) pathway, the CCB finds that entities must have obtained, or be in the process of obtaining, at least a "Basic" or "Important" level verification, or hold a signed agreement with an accredited assessment body. Under the ISO/IEC 27001 pathway, the CCB requires entities to submit the certification scope, a Statement of Applicability, and the most recent internal audit report, with full certification to be completed by April 2027. The third pathway involves direct inspection, under which the CCB requires entities to submit a self-assessment and supporting documentation and to formally request an inspection, a route the CCB notes may lead directly to supervisory measures.

The CCB states that these requirements derive from the Belgian NIS2 Law, which entered into force on 18 October 2024, and which the authority describes as aimed at strengthening the cybersecurity of essential services through risk management, incident notification and supervision. The CCB further states that failure to submit complete information within the prescribed timeframe is liable to result in administrative measures, financial penalties, or additional supervisory action. No fine amounts are specified in the published guidance.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Reports 70% Rise in Incident Notifications and Faster Exploitation Windows in 2025 Annual Review
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium recorded a 70% increase in total incident notifications in 2025, attributing the rise in part to expanded reporting obligations under NIS2 and DORA alongside sustained growth in hostile cyber activity across Belgian organisations.

On 26 March 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published its annual cyber threat review covering the 2025 reporting period. The CCB recorded 635 total incident notifications, of which 556 were classified as cyber-related, representing a 58% year-on-year increase in cyber incidents. The CCB reports that no fines were issued in connection with this report.

The CCB finds that account compromise was the most frequently reported incident category, accounting for 144 cases, with credential theft and abuse identified as the primary drivers. The CCB states that the Safeonweb BePhish platform processed approximately 10 million suspicious emails during the year, which the CCB states reflects the industrialisation of phishing campaigns. The authority notes that attackers increasingly combined email with messaging platforms, deployed CEO fraud scenarios to trigger urgent payments, and introduced techniques such as ClickFix and FileFix, in which users are manipulated into executing malicious commands directly.

On ransomware, the CCB notes that incident volumes remained relatively stable at 105 cases in 2025 compared with 109 in 2024, but finds that impact has intensified, with attackers combining data encryption, data exfiltration and escalating extortion tactics. The CCB finds that following the takedown of LockBit, the ransomware ecosystem has fragmented, with groups including Qilin, Akira and Clop identified among those most active against Belgian targets. The CCB also finds that Belgium was among the European countries most frequently targeted by pro-Russian hacktivist groups through distributed denial-of-service attacks, with group NoName057(16) launching five coordinated campaigns, often timed around geopolitical events. The authority states that the CCB's Red Button coordination procedure, enabling real-time response between victims, internet service providers, hosting providers and authorities, contributed to limiting the operational impact of these attacks.

The CCB further finds that the average time-to-exploit window between vulnerability disclosure and active exploitation fell to five days in 2025, with approximately one-third of vulnerabilities exploited within 24 hours. The authority states that the CCB conducted 103 emergency response operations and issued 32,005 targeted spear warnings, a 42% increase on the prior year.

rows.title: Bermuda PrivCom Co-Sponsors Two AI Resolutions at 47th Global Privacy Assembly in Seoul
rows.summary:
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Bermuda co-sponsored two resolutions on artificial intelligence adopted at the 47th Global Privacy Assembly in Seoul, South Korea, addressing the use of personal data in AI training and the role of human oversight in automated decision-making systems.

On 29 January 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Bermuda (PrivCom) published details of its co-sponsorship of two resolutions passed at the 47th Global Privacy Assembly (GPA), which convened in Seoul in September 2025. The GPA brings together more than 130 data protection and privacy authorities worldwide. No financial penalties are associated with the resolutions.

The first resolution, submitted by the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner and co-sponsored by PrivCom alongside authorities including the Italian Garante, the CNIL, the Data Protection Commission of Ireland, and the European Data Protection Supervisor, addresses the collection, use and disclosure of personal data to pre-train, train and fine-tune AI models. The resolution affirms that AI training is required to comply with existing privacy and data protection laws, warns against the indiscriminate scraping of publicly available personal information without a lawful condition for processing, and calls for stronger cross-border enforcement cooperation among data protection authorities to oversee global AI systems. The resolution also promotes engagement with the OECD, the Council of Europe, and UN bodies to advance AI governance frameworks. The GPA underscores, in this resolution, that privacy constitutes a fundamental human right.

The second resolution, submitted by the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada on behalf of the GPA Working Group on Ethics and Data Protection in Artificial Intelligence, addresses human oversight of automated decision-making systems. The resolution reaffirms that individuals must not be subject to fully automated decisions without meaningful human review and safeguards. The GPA finds that oversight is required to be effective, informed, and empowered rather than symbolic or procedural, and warns that automation risks undermining transparency, explainability, and accountability where individuals cannot understand or challenge system outputs. The resolution calls for clear governance frameworks enabling human intervention, override, or audit of algorithmic decisions, and highlights the need for protections where decisions affect employment, access to services, vulnerable persons, or other fundamental rights.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium Launches Second HackTheGovernment Initiative for Federal IT Systems
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) has launched the second edition of its HackTheGovernment initiative, inviting ethical hackers to identify and report vulnerabilities across federal public sector IT infrastructure under coordinated vulnerability disclosure principles.

On 13 November 2025, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium announced the opening of HackTheGovernment2025, running from 12 to 26 November 2025. The CCB states that the initiative carries no associated fine or penalty mechanism and is framed as a national cybersecurity coordination programme rather than an enforcement action.

The CCB states that participating ethical hackers are invited to test selected government systems, with named targets including the CCB itself, the defence Department, and FPS BOSA. The CCB notes that all findings identified during the two-week period are to be handled under coordinated vulnerability disclosure principles, with the CCB coordinating the process to ensure that any identified issues are addressed by the relevant public services. The CCB states that the programme is open to both emerging cybersecurity talent and experienced practitioners.

The CCB characterises collaboration with the ethical-hacking community as a strategy for enhancing public sector digital resilience, framing cybersecurity as a national responsibility rather than an isolated technical matter. The CCB further notes that the initiative is designed to encourage cooperation between government institutions, the cybersecurity community, and citizens, with the stated aim of identifying vulnerabilities before malicious actors are able to exploit them. The CCB positions the programme as building on coordinated vulnerability disclosure as an organising framework, emphasising openness and trust between public institutions and the broader security community as central to its operation.

rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium reports 2,410 NIS2 registrations and 80% rise in incident reporting since October 2024 transposition
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) has reported that 2,410 organisations from critical sectors have registered under Belgium's NIS2 transposition framework, with mandatory registration deadlines and incident reporting obligations under the Network and Information Security Directive (NIS2) producing an 80% increase in reported cyber incidents compared with the preceding period.

On 17 March 2025, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published figures showing that, since Belgium transposed the NIS2 Directive into national law in October 2024, 2,410 organisations from critical sectors including energy, transport, healthcare, digital infrastructure, government and manufacturing have completed mandatory registration at atwork.safeonweb.be. The CCB notes that a broader total of more than 4,500 organisations across all sectors have registered on the platform. Based on estimates derived from figures held by the Federal Public Service Economy, the CCB states that approximately 2,500 organisations fall within the scope of NIS2, indicating that the CCB estimates the vast majority of in-scope entities registered ahead of the deadline.

The CCB finds that incident reporting has increased materially since the NIS2 legislation came into force. The authority states that between August 2023 and September 2024, it received an average of 25 cyber incident reports per month, totalling 556 reports over 18 months. Since the introduction of NIS2 last October, the CCB records 226 incidents, representing an average of 45 reports per month. The CCB attributes this increase to the statutory reporting obligations introduced by NIS2 rather than to any demonstrated rise in the volume of cyber attacks on Belgian organisations, noting that prior to transposition there was underreporting by affected entities.

The CCB states that registered organisations, including those outside the mandatory NIS2 scope, receive access to a range of services including priority vulnerability alerts, cyber threat notifications, quick scan security reports, self-assessment tools and policy templates. The CCB states that NIS2-designated organisations receive priority status in the CCB's alerting processes. The authority's Director General, Miguel De Bruycker, states that organisations are actively working to raise their cybersecurity posture and that registration and incident report volumes are expected to increase further before stabilising.

rows.title: Belgium publishes list of 26 fundamental-rights authorities under EU AI Act Article 77
rows.summary:
Belgium has published its list of 26 authorities designated under Article 77 of the EU AI Act for fundamental-rights protection. The list, hosted by SPF Economie and last updated 11 March 2025, satisfies the Article 77(2) obligation requiring each Member State to identify and publicly disclose its fundamental-rights authorities by 2 November 2024.

The Article 77 obligation entered into force across the EU on 2 November 2024 under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689. Belgium's federal Ministère de l'Économie (SPF Economie) hosts the official PDF on its Intelligence Artificielle topic page. The PDF was last updated on 11 March 2025.

Confirmed designated bodies include Unia (anti-discrimination, the federal interfederal centre for equal opportunities) and Myria (the Federal Migration Centre). Per Unia's own announcement of 28 March 2025, Belgium has named 26 federal and regional regulators under this designation.

Operational note: Article 77 authorities are not Competent Authorities under Article 70. They do not enforce the AI Act directly. Instead, they receive Article 77 powers — to request and access any AI Act documentation in accessible form, and to request technical testing of high-risk AI systems through the relevant market surveillance authority — to support their existing fundamental-rights mandates.

Source: SPF Economie 'Intelligence artificielle' page (https://economie.fgov.be/fr/themes/line/intelligence-artificielle), confirmed by Unia announcement of 28 March 2025.
